content:
# Exodus Chapter 5 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~33v–34r (PDF pp. 65–66); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 45–46 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_005_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_005_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_005_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_005_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** And afterward Moses and Aaron went and said to Pharaoh, "Thus says the LORD, the God of Israel: 'Let My people go, that they may hold a feast to Me in the wilderness.'"

**2** And Pharaoh said, "Who is the LORD, that I should listen to His voice to send Israel out? I do not know the LORD, and moreover I will not send Israel out."

**3** And they said, "The God of the Hebrews has met with us. Let us go, we pray, a journey of three days into the wilderness, and let us sacrifice to the LORD our God, lest He fall upon us with pestilence or with the sword."

**4** And the king of Egypt said to them, "Why, Moses and Aaron, do you cause the people to cease from their labors? Go to your burdens!"

**5** And Pharaoh said, "Behold, the people of the land are now many, and you would cause them to cease from their burdens."

**6** And Pharaoh commanded on that day the taskmasters over the people and their foremen, saying,

**7** "You shall no longer give the people straw to make the bricks as before. Let them go and gather straw for themselves.

**8** But the quota of bricks that they were making before, you shall impose upon them; you shall not diminish from it. For they are idle; therefore they cry out, saying, 'Let us go and sacrifice to our God.'

**9** Let the labor be heavy upon the men, and let them work at it, and let them not pay attention to words of falsehood."

**10** And the taskmasters of the people and their foremen went out and spoke to the people, saying, "Thus says Pharaoh: 'I am not giving you straw.

**11** You go, get yourselves straw from wherever you can find it, for nothing of your labor will be diminished.'"

**12** And the people scattered throughout all the land of Egypt to gather stubble for straw.

**13** And the taskmasters were pressing them, saying, "Complete your work, the daily matter in its day, just as when there was straw."

**14** And the foremen of the sons of Israel, whom the taskmasters of Pharaoh had set over them, were beaten, saying, "Why have you not completed your prescribed amount to make bricks, both yesterday and today, as before?"

**15** And the foremen of the sons of Israel came and cried out to Pharaoh, saying, "Why do you deal thus with your servants?

**16** No straw is given to your servants, yet they say to us, 'Make bricks!' And behold, your servants are being beaten, but the sin is upon your own people."

**17** And he said, "Idle you are, idle! Therefore you are saying, 'Let us go and sacrifice to the LORD.'

**18** And now, go, work! And straw will not be given to you, but the full number of bricks you shall deliver."

**19** And the foremen of the sons of Israel saw themselves in an evil plight when it was said, "You shall not diminish from your bricks, the daily matter in its day."

**20** And they met Moses and Aaron, who were standing to meet them as they came out from before Pharaoh.

**21** And they said to them, "May the LORD look upon you and judge, for you have made our scent odious in the eyes of Pharaoh and in the eyes of his servants, putting a sword in their hand to kill us."

**22** And Moses returned to the LORD and said, "Lord, why have You brought evil upon this people? Why is it that You have sent me?

**23** For since I came to Pharaoh to speak in Your name, he has done evil to this people, and You have certainly not delivered Your people."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations
The Leningrad Codex folio ~33v (PDF p. 65) contains the beginning of Exodus 5, which follows immediately after the conclusion of chapter 4 (v. 31) on the same page. The three-column layout continues with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation accents. The text flows onto folio ~34r (PDF p. 66), completing the chapter and transitioning into chapter 6. Masorah parva annotations are visible in the margins. The parashah break (setumah) between chapters 4 and 5 is discernible. The Codex Vaticanus (pp. 45-46) was consulted as the primary Greek witness. On page 45, the text of Exodus 5 begins in the third column (following the conclusion of chapter 4). The continuous uncial script without word division runs through page 46, where the chapter concludes and chapter 6 begins. The Vaticanus text generally follows the MT in structure and content for this chapter, with minor expansions noted below.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"Let My people go" (v. 1):** Hebrew shallach et-ammi. The first occurrence of this iconic demand, which will recur throughout the plague narratives. The verb shallach (Piel) means "to send away, release, let go." The construction uses the imperative with the direct object marker et, emphasizing that the people belong to God (ammi, "My people"), not to Pharaoh. LOGOS renders "Let My people go" in accord with the traditional English rendering, which faithfully captures the imperative force. The LXX reads exaposteilon ton laon mou ("send away my people"), using the compound verb for emphasis.

2. **"Who is the LORD?" (v. 2):** Hebrew mi YHWH asher eshma beqolo. Pharaoh's response is not merely ignorance but defiance. The interrogative mi ("who?") dismisses YHWH's authority, and the relative clause "that I should listen to His voice" reduces the divine command to a matter of Pharaoh's discretion. The verb yada ("know") in "I do not know the LORD" carries covenantal weight -- to "know" in Hebrew is to recognize, to acknowledge relationship and authority. Pharaoh's denial is a rejection of YHWH's sovereignty. The LXX renders ouk oida ton kurion ("I do not know the Lord"), preserving the verb's force.

3. **"The God of the Hebrews has met with us" (v. 3):** Hebrew Elohey ha'Ivrim niqra alenu. The verb niqra (Niphal of q-r-h, "to encounter, meet") conveys an uninvited divine encounter. The shift from "the LORD, the God of Israel" (v. 1) to "the God of the Hebrews" (v. 3) is significant: when addressing Pharaoh the second time, Moses and Aaron use the ethnonym ha'Ivrim ("the Hebrews"), which is the external designation used by and for foreigners. This diplomatic recasting does not diminish the theological claim but frames it in terms Pharaoh might recognize. LOGOS preserves the shift.

4. **"Go to your burdens" (v. 4):** Hebrew lekhu lesivloteykhem. The noun sivlot ("burdens, forced labor") is a key term in the Exodus narrative, first appearing in 1:11 and recurring in 2:11, 5:4-5, and 6:6-7. It derives from the root s-b-l ("to bear, carry a burden") and specifically denotes the compulsory labor imposed on a subject population. LOGOS maintains "burdens" throughout as the consistent rendering.

5. **"The people of the land are now many" (v. 5):** Hebrew hen rabbim attah am ha'arets. This clause echoes the anxiety of 1:9 ("the people of the sons of Israel are more and many than we"). Pharaoh perceives the Israelite population as a threat, and any cessation of labor compounds the danger. The phrase am ha'arets ("people of the land") here refers to the laboring populace, though in later biblical usage it acquires other connotations. LOGOS translates directly.

6. **"Taskmasters" and "foremen" (v. 6):** Hebrew nogesim and shoterim. Two distinct levels of supervision are identified: the nogesim ("taskmasters, drivers") are Egyptian overseers who enforce the labor regime, while the shoterim ("foremen, officers") are Israelites appointed to organize the work gangs among their own people. This two-tier system is critical to the narrative: when the quota cannot be met, it is the Israelite foremen (shoterim) who are beaten (v. 14), creating internal conflict within the Israelite community. LOGOS consistently renders nogesim as "taskmasters" and shoterim as "foremen."

7. **"Straw" and "stubble" (vv. 7, 12):** Hebrew teven and qash. The word teven ("straw, chopped straw") refers to the processed material mixed into mud-bricks as a binding agent. When the Israelites are forced to gather their own, they collect qash ("stubble," the dried stalks left standing in the field after harvest). LOGOS distinguishes between the two: "straw" for teven (the finished product) and "stubble" for qash (the raw material gathered in the field, v. 12). The LXX uses achura ("chaff/straw") for both, collapsing the distinction.

8. **"Words of falsehood" (v. 9):** Hebrew divrey shaqer. Pharaoh characterizes the divine command relayed by Moses as "words of falsehood" -- deceptive speech. The noun sheqer ("falsehood, deception, lie") is a strong term used elsewhere for false witness (Exodus 20:16, Deuteronomy 5:20) and false prophecy (Jeremiah 23:14). For Pharaoh, the God of the Hebrews is a fiction and Moses' message a fabrication. LOGOS renders literally, preserving Pharaoh's contemptuous dismissal.

9. **"Made our scent odious" (v. 21):** Hebrew hiv'ashtem et-reichenu. Literally "you have caused our smell to stink." The verb b-'-sh (Hiphil, "to make stink, make odious") combined with reach ("scent, smell") creates a vivid olfactory metaphor for destroyed reputation. The foremen accuse Moses and Aaron of making the Israelites repulsive to Pharaoh -- their standing, already low, has now become noxious. The idiom occurs also in Genesis 34:30 and 1 Samuel 13:4. The LXX reads ebdeluxate ten osmen hemon ("you have made our smell abominable"), preserving the metaphor. LOGOS renders the image literally as "made our scent odious" rather than smoothing to "made us offensive."

10. **"Putting a sword in their hand to kill us" (v. 21):** Hebrew latet cherev beyadam lahargeinu. The foremen's accusation is pointed: Moses and Aaron have, in effect, armed Pharaoh and his servants against the Israelites. The sword imagery is metaphorical but carries real danger -- the Israelites fear that Pharaoh's increased hostility will escalate from labor oppression to outright slaughter. This accusation anticipates the actual killing threat of the firstborn in chapters to come.

11. **"Why have You brought evil upon this people?" (v. 22):** Hebrew lamah hare'otah la'am hazzeh. Moses' complaint to God uses the Hiphil of r-'-' ("to do evil, bring calamity"). This is not an accusation of moral wrongdoing but a lament over the harmful outcome of the mission. The verb hare'otah directly attributes the suffering to God's agency -- Moses does not blame Pharaoh but the One who sent him. This raw theological honesty is characteristic of the lament tradition (cf. Jeremiah 20:7, Habakkuk 1:2-4). LOGOS preserves "brought evil" as a formal equivalent, maintaining the theological weight.

12. **"You have certainly not delivered" (v. 23):** Hebrew vehatsel lo-hitsalta. The infinitive absolute construction (hatsel + hitsalta) conveys emphatic negation: "delivering, You have not delivered." This intensified denial closes the chapter on a note of profound disillusionment. God promised deliverance (3:8), yet the situation has worsened. Moses' words set the stage for God's response in chapter 6, where the divine name YHWH is reaffirmed and the covenant promises restated. The LXX renders ou(de) errusw ("nor have You rescued"), losing the emphatic doubling. LOGOS uses "certainly not delivered" to capture the intensifying force of the infinitive absolute.

13. **"The sin is upon your own people" (v. 16):** Hebrew vechatat ammekha. This phrase is textually and interpretively complex. The word chatat can mean "sin" or "fault." The foremen protest to Pharaoh that the blame lies with his own administration, not with the Israelite workers. Some render "you wrong your own people" (taking am as referring to the Egyptians) while others take it as "the fault is in your own people" (the taskmasters). The LXX reads adikeseis oun ton laon sou ("you will wrong your people"), interpreting chatat as an action. LOGOS renders "the sin is upon your own people" to preserve the noun form and the ambiguity of reference.

14. **"Idle you are, idle!" (v. 17):** Hebrew nirpim attem nirpim. The Niphal participle of r-p-h ("to be slack, idle, weak") is doubled for emphasis. The same root appears in 4:26 where God "relented" (raphah) from Moses. Here Pharaoh applies it contemptuously: the Israelites are "slack" -- their desire to worship is, in his eyes, a symptom of laziness, not devotion. The ironic reversal is sharp: what God's people call worship, Pharaoh calls idleness. LOGOS preserves the emphatic doubling.

### Consistent Term Renderings (Exodus Series)
| Hebrew | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| bney Yisra'el | sons of Israel | Not "children of Israel" or "Israelites" |
| Par'oh | Pharaoh | Retained as title; also "king of Egypt" in v. 4 |
| YHWH | the LORD | Small-caps convention; represents the Tetragrammaton |
| Elohim | God | When referring to the God of Israel |
| shallach (Piel) | let go / send out | Context-dependent: "let My people go" (v. 1) |
| nogesim | taskmasters | Egyptian overseers of forced labor |
| shoterim | foremen | Israelite officers within the labor gangs |
| sivlot | burdens | Forced labor; recurring term from 1:11 |
| teven | straw | Processed binding material for bricks |
| qash | stubble | Raw field stalks gathered as substitute for straw |
| levenah / levenim | brick / bricks | Mud-brick construction; matkon = "prescribed amount" |
| sheqer | falsehood | Strong term: deception, lying; v. 9 |
| hiv'ish (Hiphil) | made odious | Lit. "made to stink"; v. 21 |
| hatsel hitsalta | certainly delivered | Infinitive absolute + finite verb = emphasis |
| nirpim | idle | Niphal of r-p-h: slack, idle; doubled in v. 17 |

— LOGOS
